summary: DeVault describes buying a 1984 Dodge D250 truck to avoid the proprietary computerized components of modern vehicles, then documents designing and 3D printing a replacement plastic part using solvespace CAD software. He chose an older truck as a functional tool, prioritizing repairability and simplicity over creature comforts. The post walks through his iterative design process of measuring the broken part, printing prototypes, and refining the fit. He shares the CAD files in a public SourceHut repository, treating even his truck repairs as open-source projects.
key_points: Modern cars use proprietary components, cryptography, and legal threats to prevent end-user repair, pushing DeVault toward a simpler 1984 truck
He views vehicles strictly as functional tools and chose one he could learn to maintain himself
He used solvespace (open-source CAD) and 3D printing to design a replacement part through iterative prototyping
The CAD files are published in a public SourceHut repository, extending open-source principles to physical objects
money_quotes: I think of a vehicle strictly as a functional tool.
Modern cars are very complicated machines, and many have lots of proprietary computerized components which make end-user maintenance very difficult.
Sometimes manufacturers even use cryptography and legal threats to bring cars into their dealerships, bullying out third-party repairs.
To avoid these, I got an older truck: a 1984 Dodge D250.
It's a much simpler machine than most modern cars, and learning how to repair and maintain it is something I can do in my spare time.
key_insight: The same principles that drive free software advocacy — repairability, user autonomy, and rejection of proprietary lock-in — apply equally to choosing and maintaining a vehicle.
